concerns.text: SC-001 states the bound is 'measured against the mathematical validity' of the model. This is circular and unmeasurable. A derivation is either mathematically correct or incorrect; it is not 'measured' against itself. The SC must specify the verification procedure (e.g., 'The derivation steps are verified by a symbolic math engine or peer review checklist confirming algebraic consistency').

SC-002 claims the 'empirical convergence failure point' is measured against the 'theoretical threshold'. This is unmeasurable because the spec does not define the 'failure point' metric. Is it the first N where p-value < 0.05? The first N where variance exceeds X? Without a defined metric for 'failure', the comparison cannot be executed.

SC-003 states stability is 'measured against the full-batch variance baseline'. This is vague. 'Stability' is not a defined metric. The SC must specify the exact comparison metric (e.g., 'The ratio of heuristic variance to full-batch variance is calculated; stability is confirmed if this ratio remains within [0.9, 1.1] for 95% of steps').

SC-004 mentions measuring 'variation in false-positive rates' but the spec never defines what constitutes a 'false positive' in this context (e.g., heuristic claiming stability when it is not?). Without a definition of the ground truth for 'false positive', this metric cannot be calculated or verified.

FR-006 requires a 'paired t-test... against the theoretical lower bound'. A t-test compares two distributions of empirical data. Comparing empirical data against a single theoretical scalar/bound is a one-sample test or a goodness-of-fit test, not a paired t-test. The requirement describes a statistically invalid procedure, making it unverifiable as written.
